title: ES6+ Features Overview
tldr: ES6 (ES2015) was a major update to JavaScript, introducing classes, modules, arrow functions, promises, template literals, destructuring, spread/rest, let/const, and more.
Subsequent yearly releases (ES2016–ES2024) added async/await, optional chaining, nullish coalescing, array methods, and other improvements.
ES6+ features make JavaScript code more readable, maintainable, and powerful — they are essential for modern development.

Key themes: better syntax (arrow functions, template literals), better organization (modules, classes), better async (promises, async/await), and better data handling (Map, Set, Symbol, typed arrays).
laymanDefinition: Imagine you've been driving a car from 1995. It works, but it lacks modern features — no GPS, no backup camera, no Bluetooth. Then, in 2015, the car company releases a massive upgrade: GPS navigation, voice control, automatic parking, and smartphone integration. That's ES6. And every year since, they add smaller improvements — better voice recognition, lane assist, etc. (ES2016 through ES2024). You can still drive the old car, but the new one makes everything easier, safer, and more enjoyable. That's what ES6+ does for JavaScript.

deepDive.heading: ES6 Core Features (2015)
deepDive.text: ES6 introduced: let/const (block-scoped variables), arrow functions (shorter syntax, lexical this), classes (syntactic sugar over prototypes), template literals (interpolated strings), destructuring (extract values from arrays/objects), spread/rest operators, default parameters, enhanced object literals, for...of loops, Map/Set/WeakMap/WeakSet, Symbol, Promises, generators (function*), modules (import/export), iterators, and Proxy/Reflect.

deepDive.heading: ES2016–ES2017 — Async Revolution
deepDive.text: ES2016 added Array.prototype.includes and the exponentiation operator (**). ES2017 was a landmark year with async/await (syntactic sugar over Promises), Object.values/Object.entries, string padding (padStart/padEnd), trailing commas in function parameters, and SharedArrayBuffer/Atomics.

deepDive.heading: ES2018–ES2020 — Object and Async Improvements
deepDive.text: ES2018 added rest/spread for objects, Promise.prototype.finally, async iteration (for await...of), and RegExp improvements. ES2019 added Array.flat/flatMap, Object.fromEntries, and optional catch binding. ES2020 introduced the nullish coalescing operator (??), optional chaining (?.), BigInt, globalThis, dynamic import, Promise.allSettled, and String.matchAll.

deepDive.heading: ES2021–ES2024 — Ongoing Refinements
deepDive.text: ES2021 added String.replaceAll, Promise.any, logical assignment operators (&&=, ||=, ??=), and numeric separators. ES2022 added class fields (public/private), top-level await, Array/RegExp.hasIndices, and static class blocks. ES2023 added Array.findLast/findLastIndex, Hashbang grammar, and immutable array methods (toSorted, toReversed, toSpliced, with). ES2024 added groupBy (Map.groupBy, Object.groupBy), Promise.withResolvers, and RegExp v flag.

deepDive.heading: Modern JavaScript — Transpilation and Polyfills
deepDive.text: While modern engines support most ES6+ features, production code often uses transpilers (Babel) to convert modern syntax to ES5 for older browser support. Polyfills (core-js) provide runtime implementations for missing features. However, modern development increasingly targets evergreen browsers, reducing the need for transpilation.

interviewAnswer: ES6 (ES2015) was a transformative update to JavaScript, introducing let/const, arrow functions, classes, template literals, destructuring, spread/rest, modules, Promises, generators, Map/Set/Symbol, and more. Subsequent yearly releases added async/await (ES2017), async iteration and rest/spread for objects (ES2018), flat/flatMap and Object.fromEntries (ES2019), optional chaining and nullish coalescing (ES2020), logical assignment operators and replaceAll (ES2021), class fields and top-level await (ES2022), findLast and immutable array methods (ES2023), and groupBy with Promise.withResolvers (ES2024). Key themes across releases: better syntax ergonomics, improved async patterns, enhanced data structures, and more expressive object/array operations.

interviewQuestions.question: What major features did ES6 (ES2015) introduce?
interviewQuestions.answer: let/const, arrow functions, classes, template literals, destructuring, spread/rest, default parameters, enhanced object literals, for...of, Map/Set/WeakMap/WeakSet, Symbol, Promises, generators, modules, iterators, Proxy/Reflect.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between let, const, and var?
interviewQuestions.answer: var is function-scoped and hoisted. let/const are block-scoped and not hoisted (TDZ). const prevents reassignment but not mutation of objects/arrays.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the arrow function's key difference from regular functions?
interviewQuestions.answer: Arrow functions have lexical 'this' (inherited from enclosing scope), cannot be used as constructors (no new), and have no arguments object.

interviewQuestions.question: What does optional chaining (?.) do?
interviewQuestions.answer: Optional chaining safely accesses nested properties: obj?.prop?.nested. Returns undefined if any intermediate value is null/undefined instead of throwing.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the nullish coalescing operator (??)?
interviewQuestions.answer: It returns the right-hand side only if the left-hand side is null or undefined, not for other falsy values like 0, '', or false.

interviewQuestions.question: What did ES2017 add?
interviewQuestions.answer: async/await, Object.values/Object.entries, string padding (padStart/padEnd), trailing commas in function parameters.

interviewQuestions.question: What did ES2020 add?
interviewQuestions.answer: Optional chaining (?.), nullish coalescing (??), BigInt, globalThis, dynamic import, Promise.allSettled, String.matchAll.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between Promise.all and Promise.allSettled?
interviewQuestions.answer: Promise.all rejects immediately if any promise rejects. Promise.allSettled waits for all promises to settle (resolve or reject) and returns results with status.

interviewQuestions.question: What did ES2022 add?
interviewQuestions.answer: Class fields (public/private with #), top-level await (await at module top level without async function), static class blocks, and RegExp.hasIndices.

interviewQuestions.question: What did ES2023 add?
interviewQuestions.answer: Array.findLast/findLastIndex, immutable array methods (toSorted, toReversed, toSpliced, with), and Hashbang grammar (#! for CLI scripts).

codeExamples.title: ES6 — let/const, Arrow Functions, Template Literals
codeExamples.description: ES6 introduced let/const for block scoping, arrow functions for concise syntax with lexical this, template literals for interpolation.

codeExamples.title: ES6 — Destructuring and Spread/Rest
codeExamples.description: Destructuring extracts values from arrays/objects. Spread expands iterables. Rest collects remaining parameters or properties.

codeExamples.title: ES6+ — Promises and async/await
codeExamples.description: Promises (ES6) provide structured async handling. async/await (ES2017) makes async code read like synchronous. Later ES versions added Promise combinators.

codeExamples.title: ES2020 — Optional Chaining and Nullish Coalescing
codeExamples.useCase: Safe property access and default values
codeExamples.description: Optional chaining (?.) safely accesses nested properties. Nullish coalescing (??) provides defaults only for null/undefined, not other falsy values.

codeExamples.title: ES2022–2024 — Recent Additions
codeExamples.description: Recent ES versions added private class fields, top-level await, immutable array methods, findLast, Object.groupBy, and Promise.withResolvers.

mcqQuestions.question: What year was ES6 (major JavaScript update) released?
mcqQuestions.explanation: ES6 (also called ES2015) was released in 2015 and was the most significant update to JavaScript.

mcqQuestions.question: Which feature was NOT introduced in ES6?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Async/await was introduced in ES2017 (ES8). ES6 introduced arrow functions, let/const, classes, Promises, modules, etc.

mcqQuestions.question: What is the key difference between var and let?
options: var is block-scoped, let is function-scoped
let is block-scoped, var is function-scoped
mcqQuestions.explanation: let is block-scoped (visible only within the block). var is function-scoped (visible throughout the function).

mcqQuestions.question: What does the optional chaining operator (?.) do?
options: Returns undefined if any intermediate value is null/undefined
mcqQuestions.explanation: Optional chaining (?.) safely accesses nested properties, returning undefined if any intermediate value is null or undefined.

mcqQuestions.question: What does the nullish coalescing operator (??) do?
options: Returns right side for any falsy value
Returns right side only for null/undefined
mcqQuestions.explanation: ?? returns the right operand only if the left operand is null or undefined, not for other falsy values like 0 or ''.

mcqQuestions.question: Which ES version introduced async/await?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Async/await was introduced in ES2017 as part of the async revolution in JavaScript.

mcqQuestions.question: What does ES2023's toSorted() do differently from sort()?
options: It returns a new sorted array without mutating the original
mcqQuestions.explanation: toSorted() returns a new sorted array and does not modify the original. sort() mutates the original array in place.

mcqQuestions.question: What is the difference between Promise.all and Promise.allSettled?
options: Promise.all rejects on first failure; Promise.allSettled waits for all to complete
Promise.all accepts arrays; Promise.allSettled accepts objects
mcqQuestions.explanation: Promise.all short-circuits on first rejection. Promise.allSettled waits for all promises to settle and returns results with status.

mcqQuestions.question: How do you declare a private field in an ES2022 class?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Private class fields use the # prefix: #field. They are truly private and inaccessible outside the class.

mcqQuestions.question: What does ES2024's Object.groupBy() do?
options: Groups array elements by a key function
mcqQuestions.explanation: Object.groupBy() groups elements of an iterable based on a callback function that returns the group key.
